---
title: "Final Submission"
format: html
toc: true
draft: true
---

<!-- 
  DRAFT — This page is not yet linked in the website navigation.
  Review and update the content below before publishing.
-->

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
**Due: May 12** · Submit via Gradescope (one teammate submits; include both names)
:::

---

## Deliverables

| Item | Format | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Final report** | PDF, 6–10 pages | Conference-style (see structure below) |
| **Code repository** | GitHub link | Must include README with reproduction instructions |
| **Presentation** | Slides (PDF or PPTX) | 10–12 minutes + Q&A |

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
All deliverables should be submitted as a **single Gradescope submission** with the report PDF as the primary file and the repo link included in the report.
:::

---

## Report structure

Your final report should follow this outline:

### 1. Abstract
One paragraph summarizing the problem, approach, and key findings.

### 2. Introduction & motivation
What problem are you solving? Why does it matter? How does it relate to course topics?

### 3. Related work
Brief survey of relevant methods. Highlight what you build on and what you do differently.

### 4. Method
Describe your approach clearly enough that a classmate could reproduce it. Include:

- Pipeline overview (diagrams encouraged)
- Key algorithms and design choices
- Assumptions and limitations

### 5. Experiments & results

- **Setup:** datasets, hardware, parameters, baselines
- **Metrics:** clearly defined and justified
- **Results:** tables, figures, qualitative examples
- **Ablations:** what components matter and why

### 6. Discussion
Interpret your results. What worked? What didn't? Why? Include failure-case analysis.

### 7. Conclusion
Summary of contributions, limitations, and potential future work.

### 8. References
Cite all external resources: papers, code, datasets, libraries.

---

## Presentation guidelines

- **Duration:** 10–12 minutes + 3–5 minutes Q&A.
- **Content:** Focus on motivation, method, key results, and lessons learned.
- **Demos:** Live or recorded demos are encouraged but optional.
- All team members should present.

---

## Grading rubric

| Category | Weight | What we look for |
|---|---:|---|
| **Technical correctness** | 30% | Sound formulation, correct implementation |
| **Results & evaluation** | 30% | Meaningful experiments, fair comparisons, ablations |
| **Clarity & communication** | 20% | Well-structured report, readable figures, clear presentation |
| **Originality & ambition** | 10% | Thoughtful problem choice, extensions beyond baseline |
| **Reproducibility** | 10% | Clean repo, clear README, documented dependencies |

---

## Submission checklist

- [ ] Report is 6–10 pages, follows the structure above
- [ ] All figures are labeled, captioned, and readable
- [ ] Code repo link is included and accessible
- [ ] README has setup, data, run, and output instructions
- [ ] Presentation slides are ready
- [ ] Both team members' names are on the submission
